title: The Input and Candidate Gates
concept: The input gate decides which new information to store in the cell state, while the candidate gate creates a vector of new candidate values.

exercises.prompt: Which two gates work together in an LSTM to add new information to the cell memory?
exercises.explain: The input gate decides which new information gets stored in the cell state, and the candidate gate creates the vector of new candidate values.

cards.title: Filtering the New stuff
cards.body: When you read a new page of a book, how do you decide what new details to remember and what to ignore? LSTMs do this using two special gates that team up to screen all incoming information!

cards.title: Meet the Two Gates
cards.body: In an LSTM network, the input gate and the candidate gate work as a team. The candidate gate creates a set of new, potential values to remember. At the same time, the input gate decides exactly which parts of those new values are important enough to save into the long-term memory.

cards.title: The Shopping Cart Filter
cards.body: Think of updating your shopping list. The candidate gate is like brainstorming a list of exciting new snacks you could buy. The input gate is like a strict budget filter, deciding which of those brainstormed snacks are actually worth adding to your shopping cart.

cards.title: Reading a Movie Review
cards.body: Imagine an AI reading a movie review. When it reads 'The special effects were amazing, but the plot was terrible', the candidate gate suggests adding 'effects: amazing' and 'plot: terrible' to memory. The input gate, knowing the review is about the plot, decides to save 'plot: terrible' and ignore the effects.

cards.title: The Filter vs. The Creator
cards.body: People often think the input gate creates the new data itself. In reality, it only acts as a filter. The candidate gate is the one that actually creates the new options, while the input gate just says 'yes' or 'no' to them.

cards.title: Creator vs. Gatekeeper
cards.body: Here is how the roles of the two gates compare when handling new information.

right.text: Decides how much of each new value to let through (scaled from 0 to 1)

left.text: Creates new potential values to remember (scaled from -1 to 1)

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of the candidate gate in an LSTM?

options.text: It creates a set of new candidate values to potentially add to memory.
It deletes the old memory entirely.
It decides what information to output to the user.
It controls the computer's physical memory chips.

exercises.explain: The candidate gate uses a tanh activation function, which outputs values between -1 and 1, to prepare new candidate values for the memory.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The input gate decides which parts of the candidate gate's output actually get stored in memory.
exercises.explain: This is true! The input gate acts as a selector, deciding which parts of the candidate gate's new information are worth keeping.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing gate that acts as the filter.
exercises.sentence: While the candidate gate suggests new information, the [blank] gate decides if it gets saved.
exercises.explain: The input gate acts as the filter, determining which of the newly proposed candidate values will be allowed into the long-term memory.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between the two gates?

options.text: The candidate gate proposes new info, and the input gate filters it.
Both gates do the exact same job of erasing old memories.
The input gate creates new info, and the candidate gate filters it.

exercises.explain: Option A is correct because the candidate gate creates the new options (using tanh), while the input gate filters them (using sigmoid). Option B mistakenly says they do the same thing, and Option C gets their roles reversed.

exercises.prompt: How do the input gate and the candidate gate work together to update an LSTM's memory?

options.text: The input gate creates the new ideas, and the candidate gate blocks them.
The candidate gate creates the new ideas, and the input gate decides which ones to save.
Both gates erase the entire memory to start fresh.
The input gate decides what to forget from the past.

exercises.explain: The candidate gate generates a new set of possible values (scaled between -1 and 1), and the input gate acts as a valve (scaled between 0 and 1) to determine how much of that candidate information actually makes it into the cell memory.
